verses.text: Jonathan, telling no one, proposes to cross to the Philistine garrison with only his armourbearer, between two steep crags. Faith does not always wait for permission or numbers; it sees the LORD's opportunity and moves. He attempts the impossible because he believes God is in it.

Jonathan, telling no one, proposes to cross to the Philistine garrison with only his armourbearer, between two steep crags. Faith does not always wait for permission or numbers; it sees the LORD's opportunity and moves. He attempts the impossible because he believes God is in it.

'There is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few,' Jonathan says. He will act, yet leaves the result to God: 'it may be that the LORD will work for us.' His armourbearer answers, 'I am with thee.' Bold faith and loyal company make a mighty pair.

'There is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few,' Jonathan says. He will act, yet leaves the result to God: 'it may be that the LORD will work for us.' His armourbearer answers, 'I am with thee.' Bold faith and loyal company make a mighty pair.

Jonathan sets a sign: if the Philistines call them up, it is God's go-ahead. He does not presume, but seeks a token of the LORD's will before he climbs. True faith is daring yet humble — ready to act, but watching for God's hand to point the way.

Jonathan sets a sign: if the Philistines call them up, it is God's go-ahead. He does not presume, but seeks a token of the LORD's will before he climbs. True faith is daring yet humble — ready to act, but watching for God's hand to point the way.

Jonathan sets a sign: if the Philistines call them up, it is God's go-ahead. He does not presume, but seeks a token of the LORD's will before he climbs. True faith is daring yet humble — ready to act, but watching for God's hand to point the way.

The Philistines mock, 'come up,' and Jonathan climbs on hands and feet into the enemy post, his armourbearer behind, and they cut down some twenty men. 'The LORD hath delivered them.' Two men with God rout a garrison; the victory is His, the courage theirs.

Suddenly the whole Philistine camp shakes — 'a very great trembling,' an earthquake from God — and Saul's watchmen see the great host melting away in confusion. When the LORD fights, He needs no army; He can scatter thousands with terror sent straight from heaven.

Suddenly the whole Philistine camp shakes — 'a very great trembling,' an earthquake from God — and Saul's watchmen see the great host melting away in confusion. When the LORD fights, He needs no army; He can scatter thousands with terror sent straight from heaven.

Saul musters and finds Jonathan gone. He calls for the ark to enquire of God, but as the Philistine uproar grows, he abruptly cancels — 'Withdraw thine hand.' Too impatient to wait on God even now, Saul rushes to act while the moment to seek Him slips away.

The Philistines are in such confusion they turn their swords on one another, and the Hebrews who had hidden now join the rout. 'So the LORD saved Israel that day.' What one man's faith began, God spread into a national victory. He delights to use small beginnings of trust.

But Saul binds the army with a rash oath: cursed is any who eats before evening. The famished men press on faint, and pass dripping honeycomb untouched. A leader's foolish vow can starve the very people he means to help; zeal without wisdom wounds its own side.

But Saul binds the army with a rash oath: cursed is any who eats before evening. The famished men press on faint, and pass dripping honeycomb untouched. A leader's foolish vow can starve the very people he means to help; zeal without wisdom wounds its own side.

But Saul binds the army with a rash oath: cursed is any who eats before evening. The famished men press on faint, and pass dripping honeycomb untouched. A leader's foolish vow can starve the very people he means to help; zeal without wisdom wounds its own side.

Jonathan, who did not hear the oath, tastes a little honey and his eyes brighten. Told of the curse, he says plainly the oath has 'troubled the land' and weakened the victory. Sometimes the wisest in the camp must name a leader's folly for what it is.

Weak with hunger, the people at last fall on the spoil and eat the meat with the blood, sinning against God's law. Saul, alarmed, rolls up a great stone and builds an altar. His rash rule led straight to the people's sin — bad commands breed worse disorder.

Saul would pursue by night, but when the priest says, 'let us draw near to God,' the LORD gives no answer. Sensing hidden sin, Saul vows the guilty shall die, 'though it be Jonathan.' He is quick to bind others with oaths, slow to see his own fault.

By lot the matter falls to Jonathan, who freely confesses he tasted a little honey. Saul, trapped by his own rash vow, declares, 'thou shalt surely die.' His pride would sooner kill his brave son than admit the oath was foolish. Stubbornness hardens into cruelty.

But the people rise: 'Shall Jonathan die, who hath wrought this great salvation in Israel? God forbid.' And they rescue him. The man God had used, the people would not let perish for a king's foolish word. Sometimes love and good sense must overrule misguided authority.

Saul does win real victories — against Moab, Ammon, Edom, and especially the Amalekites — delivering Israel from those who plundered them. For all his flaws, God uses him to save his people for a season. Even a failing leader may accomplish good while God's patience lasts.

Saul does win real victories — against Moab, Ammon, Edom, and especially the Amalekites — delivering Israel from those who plundered them. For all his flaws, God uses him to save his people for a season. Even a failing leader may accomplish good while God's patience lasts.

The chapter closes with Saul's family and his fierce, lifelong war with the Philistines, pressing every strong man he saw into service. The reign that began with promise settles into endless conflict. A life out of step with God knows little rest, only struggle.
